text.title: Automation Candidate Assessment and Adoption Gate
text.summary: Automation readiness scoring criteria, RPA vs intelligent automation comparison, and six-criterion adoption gate for deciding whether to proceed with a process-mining initiative.

section.title: Automation Candidate Scoring

rule.statement: Score automation candidates on six criteria before selecting a technology; do not target automation based on stakeholder enthusiasm.
rule.rationale: RPA initiatives that skip structured scoring fail at high rates — most failures trace to poor process understanding and incorrect assumption that the process is rule-based.

list.item: Standardization (high weight): rule-based with low exception rate.
Stability (medium weight): process unchanged for >= 6 months.
Digital Input (medium weight): structured data available, not image/PDF only.
Error Rate (medium weight): high manual error frequency indicates automation value.
ROI Potential (high weight): high labor cost, quick payback period.

example.note: Score each criterion 1-5. Total > 24 of 30: full automation. 18-24: partial automation. 12-18: process improvement first, then re-assess. < 12: not suitable for automation.

section.reference: Automation readiness scoring template with ROI estimate skeleton.

rule.statement: Distinguish RPA (rule-based, repetitive, structured data only) from Intelligent Automation (AI-driven decisions, structured and unstructured data, continuous improvement) before tool selection.
rule.rationale: Selecting RPA for variable/complex processes leads to high exception rates and human-in-the-loop bottlenecks that negate automation benefits.

list.item: RPA: task type = rule-based and repetitive; decision making = predefined rules; data = structured only; exceptions = human escalation; learning = none.
Intelligent Automation: task type = complex and variable; decision making = AI-driven; data = structured + unstructured; exceptions = autonomous handling; learning = continuous improvement.

rule.statement: Run six adoption criteria before committing to a process-mining initiative; if any criterion produces a no-go, name the single blocker and the cheapest experiment to unblock it.
rule.rationale: Tool procurement and initiative launch on a no-go log or an illiterate stakeholder group wastes budget with no recoverable output.

list.item: Event-log availability: can the source system export a log with case_id, activity, timestamp?
Case volume: >= 200 cases minimum; < 200 use SQL.
Stakeholder literacy: does at least one BA or process owner understand DFG/Petri net outputs?
Regulatory tolerance: does GDPR/HIPAA/other regulation permit event-log extraction and storage?
Tooling budget: open-source (pm4py) / mid-market (Apromore) / enterprise (Celonis) — align with ROI estimate.
Automation downstream dependency: is there an actual automation or redesign plan, or is mining a curiosity exercise?

antipattern.description: Conflating process mining (reconstructing flows from system logs) with task mining (desktop click recording) or with BPM (modeling desired processes).
antipattern.reason: Incorrect categorization leads to wrong tool purchase: Celonis for task-mining needs, or UiPath Task Mining for a conformance-checking goal. The budget is wasted and the initiative fails on deliverable mismatch.
